3A_Bibliographic.citation: Helmer, C. K., Al-shoaibi, A. A., Yang, J. H., Singh, G., Smith, N., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., Jackson, D. B., & Nagata, J. M. (2024). Associations between screen time and sleep disturbance in early adolescents. BMC Public Health, 24, 1234.
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Unable to fully extract all details from the PDF. Citation is approximate.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed cross-sectional data from 10,106 early adolescents aged 10-14 in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (Year 2, 2018-2020).

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Total recreational screen time (hours/day) and screen time by modality
3C_Exposure.modalities: TV/movies/videos, video games, texting, social media, video chat, browsing internet
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous hours/day (weighted average of weekday and weekend)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Total typical daily screen use was calculated as the weighted sum of the weekday and weekend average ([weekday average x 5] + [weekend average x 2])/7.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Sleep disturbance (overall sleep disturbance score and trouble falling/staying asleep)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children (SDSC, caregiver report) and KSADS DSM-5 item (youth report)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: 15.5% trouble falling/staying asleep; 28.0% overall sleep disturbance
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Adolescents were asked about having problems falling or staying asleep in the past 2 weeks when tired and wanting to sleep, which was adapted from the KSADS DSM-5. Caregivers completed a 26-item measure to assess the presence of sleep disorders (Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children). A cutoff of 39 was used to indicate the presence of sleep disturbance.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with robust standard errors to estimate adjusted risk ratios
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, study site, data collection period (pre- vs. during COVID-19 pandemic), depression
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression analyses using robust standard errors were conducted to calculate risk ratios estimating associations between screen time before bed and during the night (exposure variable) and sleep problems or disturbance (outcome variables), adjusting for confounders. Statistical significance was evaluated after implementing the Benjamini-Hochberg Procedure to address a false discovery rate of 0.05.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: TV or electronic device in bedroom
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Having a TV or electronic device in the bedroom was positively associated with self-reported trouble falling or staying asleep (adjusted risk ratio [ARR] 1.27, 95% CI 1.12-1.44)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: TV or electronic device in bedroom
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: caregiver-reported overall sleep disturbance (adjusted risk ratio 1.15, 95% CI 1.06-1.25)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Leaving phone ringer on (vs. turning phone off)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Leaving the ringer on, compared to turning the phone off, was also positively associated with a higher risk of self-reported trouble falling or staying asleep (ARR 1.23, 95% CI 1.03-1.46)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Spend time in chat rooms at bedtime (frequency)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: ARRs ranged from 1.08 (95% CI 1.01-1.15) for using a computer or laptop to study to 1.27 (95% CI 1.17-1.39) for spending time in chat rooms
higher total daily recreational screen time was also associated with trouble falling or staying asleep (ARR 1.04, 95% CI 1.03-1.05)

3G_Reporting_Quality.large_n_verbatim: The large, diverse, and population-based sample is a major strength, which gives the study great external validity.
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Some effect sizes were small, particularly for the caregiver reports of sleep disturbance.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper acknowledges small effect sizes for caregiver-reported outcomes. Risk ratios for total screen time are very small (1.02-1.04 per hour/day).

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Health care providers could educate parents and adolescents on specific screen behaviors around bedtime. Family media use plans informed by The American Academy of Pediatrics may help facilitate conversations about household screen rules, such as limiting screen use in the hour before bedtime.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: limiting screen usage around bedtime may help these populations with sleep management
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion interprets findings as indicating that bedtime-specific screen behaviors (having devices in bedroom, leaving ringer on, engaging in various screen activities before sleep) are consistently associated with worse sleep outcomes. The paper appropriately notes small effect sizes for caregiver-reported outcomes. The recommendations focus on bedtime-specific behaviors rather than total screen time, which is reasonable given the stronger associations for bedtime behaviors.
